reforms.description: The UK government has shifted its emphasis from grants towards tax incentives for R&D, which is expected to boost R&D spending, although the results are still pending evaluation.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Tax incentives are designed to encourage firms to increase their R&D investments, potentially leading to higher innovation and productivity.
reforms.status_evidence: The government has announced a shift in emphasis towards tax incentives for R&D.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant structural change in the approach to R&D funding, potentially impacting the entire innovation landscape in the UK.
reforms.source_quote: The recent shift in emphasis away from grants towards tax incentives is likely to be beneficial in boosting R&D spending.

reforms.description: The UK government intends to promote university-business collaboration to better exploit the science base, which includes streamlining university governance and increasing funding for successful collaborations.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Enhancing collaboration between universities and businesses is expected to facilitate knowledge transfer and innovation, leading to economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The government intends to promote university-business collaboration.
reforms.importance_rationale: This initiative aims to fundamentally enhance the interaction between academia and industry, which is crucial for driving innovation and economic performance.
reforms.source_quote: There is scope to exploit the strength of the science base through further promoting university-business collaboration.

reforms.description: The Government has announced its intention to consolidate and expand existing schemes for first-time buyers into a new 'Homebuy' scheme, allowing buyers to purchase a home for 50% to 75% of its value with the remainder owned by the Government or jointly with the lender.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: While it aims to assist first-time buyers, it could lead to higher house prices without increasing supply, potentially negating affordability benefits.
reforms.status_evidence: The Government recently announced its intention to consolidate and expand existing schemes for first-time buyers into a new 'Homebuy' scheme.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant change in housing policy aimed at improving access for first-time buyers, though its overall economic impact may be limited.
reforms.source_quote: The Government recently announced its intention to consolidate and expand existing schemes for first-time buyers into a new 'Homebuy' scheme.

reforms.description: The UK government aims to develop a coherent supply of childcare by integrating public and private providers, which is expected to stimulate innovation in childcare services.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By fostering a diverse childcare market, the reform encourages innovative solutions that can enhance service quality and accessibility, thereby supporting labor market participation.
reforms.status_evidence: The policy aim is to build a more coherent supply of childcare by 2008.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant structural change in childcare provision, aiming to enhance the quality and accessibility of services, which is crucial for supporting working families and promoting economic participation.
reforms.source_quote: The policy aim is to build a more coherent supply, as local authorities are given a duty to ensure sufficient childcare supply to meet the needs of families by 2008.

reforms.description: The UK government has published a ten-year plan aimed at boosting innovation performance, which includes a target to raise R&D intensity from 1.9% to 2.5% of GDP by 2014.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Increasing R&D intensity is expected to enhance productivity and innovation, contributing positively to economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The government has published the plan and set specific targets.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant structural change in the UK's approach to innovation, aiming to reverse a declining trend in R&D investment and enhance overall economic performance.
reforms.source_quote: The Government has recently published a 10-year plan for science and innovation which includes a target to raise R&D intensity from the current level of 1.9% to 2.5% of GDP by 2014.

reforms.description: The UK government introduced R&D tax incentives in 2000, aimed at encouraging private sector R&D investment by providing tax credits for eligible R&D spending, particularly benefiting SMEs with a repayable aspect for losses.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The tax incentives are expected to increase R&D intensity by reducing the cost of R&D for firms, thereby stimulating innovation and economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The tax incentives were introduced in 2000 and have been operational since then.
reforms.importance_rationale: The introduction of R&D tax incentives represents a significant shift in the UK’s approach to stimulating private sector R&D investment, with potential long-term impacts on innovation and economic performance.
reforms.source_quote: R&D tax incentives have only been introduced in 2000 for SMEs and extended to larger firms in 2002.

reforms.description: The Higher Education Innovation Fund (HEIF) was established to support the commercialization of university research and foster collaboration between academia and industry, with funding set to rise to £110 million by 2007/08.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By enhancing collaboration between universities and businesses, the HEIF aims to improve knowledge transfer, leading to increased innovation and economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The Government made a commitment that HEIF funding would rise to £110 million per year by 2007/08.
reforms.importance_rationale: The HEIF represents a significant investment in enhancing university-business collaboration, which is crucial for improving the commercialization of research.
reforms.source_quote: The HEIF money builds capacity in universities to enable greater collaboration with business.

reforms.description: The Regional Development Agencies were given a greater role in facilitating university-business collaboration, with significant investments in science and innovation amounting to £360 million in 2005/06, marking a 40% increase since 2002/03.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Enhancing collaboration between universities and businesses is expected to drive innovation and improve the commercialization of research outputs.
reforms.status_evidence: The agencies are actively investing in science and innovation as per the outlined budget.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant structural change in the role of Regional Development Agencies, enhancing their capacity to foster innovation through university-business partnerships.
reforms.source_quote: Regional Development Agencies were given a greater role in facilitating university-business collaboration, and are investing significantly in science and innovation (£360 million in 2005/06, an increase in real terms of about 40% since 2002/03).

reforms.description: The government has committed to actions based on the Roberts review to stimulate interest in science, engineering, and technology (SET) subjects, aiming to increase the number of graduates in these fields.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Increasing the number of graduates in SET fields is expected to enhance the supply of skilled labor for R&D, thereby supporting innovation.
reforms.status_evidence: The Government has committed itself to action along the lines of the review.
reforms.importance_rationale: This initiative addresses identified weaknesses in the supply of SET graduates, which is crucial for maintaining a competitive R&D environment.
reforms.source_quote: The Government has committed itself to action along the lines of the review (HMTreasury, et al., 2004).

reforms.description: The UK government introduced schemes providing direct funding for R&D to enhance innovation capabilities across various sectors.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Increases public investment in R&D, which can stimulate private sector innovation and enhance overall productivity.
reforms.status_evidence: The government has actively funded R&D initiatives since the introduction of the framework.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant commitment to enhancing the UK's R&D landscape, aiming to boost innovation and economic growth through increased funding.
reforms.source_quote: Schemes providing direct funding for R&D are essential to strengthen innovation.

reforms.description: The United Kingdom's government plans to increase investment in transport infrastructure to address years of under-investment, which may have hindered productivity growth and added to business costs.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Improving transport infrastructure is expected to enhance productivity by reducing congestion and business costs, thereby supporting economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The government's plans to increase investment in transport are discussed as a necessary step to improve productivity.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform addresses a significant issue of under-investment in infrastructure, which is crucial for enhancing productivity and economic performance.
reforms.source_quote: The government’s plans to increase investment in transport as well as the growing interest in congestion charging are examined.

reforms.description: The Atkinson review has established new principles for measuring government output and productivity, leading to improved metrics in national accounts for public services, particularly in health and education.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By improving the measurement of government output, the reform aims to enhance accountability and efficiency in public spending, potentially leading to better service delivery.
reforms.status_evidence: The final report was published in January 2005, establishing new measurement principles.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant methodological shift in how public sector productivity is measured, which can have far-reaching implications for public policy and resource allocation.
reforms.source_quote: The Atkinson review has established a set of principles to apply within the internationally accepted national accounting frameworks.

reforms.description: The introduction of graduate contributions from 2006 allows universities to charge annual tuition fees ranging from zero to £3000, providing additional resources without solely relying on public funding.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: This reform helps to alleviate credit constraints for students, thereby increasing access to higher education and enhancing the skilled workforce, which is crucial for innovation.
reforms.status_evidence: The graduate contributions were confirmed to be introduced in 2006.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant shift in funding for higher education, enhancing the financial sustainability of universities and improving access for students, which is vital for long-term economic growth.
reforms.source_quote: From 2006 universities are to charge annual tuition fees in the range from zero to £3000.

reforms.description: The UK government announced a mandatory target for departments to allocate 2.5% of their extra-mural R&D contracts to SMEs under the Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI), aimed at enhancing support for SME innovation.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: This initiative is expected to stimulate innovation among SMEs, which are crucial for economic growth and job creation.
reforms.status_evidence: The government recently announced a mandatory target for Government departments and agencies.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant step towards enhancing SME participation in R&D, addressing market failures in innovation support.
reforms.source_quote: The Government recently announced a mandatory target for Government departments and agencies to place 2.5% of their extra-mural R&D contracts with SMEs under the Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI), to provide enhanced support for SME innovation.

reforms.description: The UK government announced a wide-ranging reform of vocational programmes for students aged 14-19, introducing specialist diplomas aimed at equipping young people with work-relevant skills and improving vocational education quality.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By enhancing vocational training, the reform aims to improve the skill set of the workforce, thereby increasing employability and productivity.
reforms.status_evidence: The white paper on education and skills was published in February 2005 outlining the reform.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant shift in vocational education, aiming to enhance the quality and relevance of vocational qualifications, which is crucial for addressing skills shortages in the economy.
reforms.source_quote: The Government has announced a wide-ranging reform of vocational programmes for students aged 14-19 years.

reforms.description: The National Employer Training Programme was announced to provide training for low-skilled workers, focusing on basic literacy skills and qualifications, with public funding covering training costs.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By enhancing the skills of the workforce, the programme aims to improve overall productivity and employability, addressing skill gaps in the labor market.
reforms.status_evidence: The programme is set to be established across England from 2006-07 following successful pilots.
reforms.importance_rationale: This initiative is a major reform aimed at addressing the skills shortage in the workforce, with significant implications for productivity and economic growth.
reforms.source_quote: The Government targets a 40% reduction in the number of workforce adults having less than the basic level 2 qualification.
